*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and sentence lengths between 1 to 25 years.
::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility is Determined Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person's prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison. Sentencing judges have no discretion to set or influence the actual durations of the prison sentences they impose for each crime. “Judicial” maximum prison sentences must be fixed at the same level as the statutory maximum penalty for the offense of conviction. Any applicable minimum terms to parole-release eligibility are also preset by statute. Parole release dates are determined by the parole board.
## In Prison Past Their Parole Eligibility In 2020, there were 715 people* in prison past their parole eligibility. This group made up 34 percent of people* in prison. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: From 2010 to 2020, the percentage of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 11 percent. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
### Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 63 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Gender In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were males, representing 90 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Age In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility were between the ages of 35 to 44 years old, representing 33 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
### Offense Types In 2020, 61 percent of people* in prison past their parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 30 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (31%) and property (12%) offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
### Sentence Lengths In 2020, most people* in prison past their parole eligibility had original sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 62 percent of in prison past parole eligibility. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## State Notes "What if we change the section from Missing Data to more of a high-level state-specific Methods section? It can include: 1) Total N for the state 2) How we determined parole eligibility (e.g., what formulas did we use to estimate parole eligibility year) 3) Missing data patterns" - Jess S. # Prison Population ## Prison Population Trends From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 13 percent, changing from 6,807 in 2010 to 5,911 in 2022. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2022, most people in prison were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 57 percent of people in prison. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Gender In 2022, most people in prison were males, representing 93 percent of people in prison. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Age In 2020, most people in prison were between 35 to 44 years old, representing 32 percent of people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
## Offense Types In 2020, most people in prison were incarcerated for rape or sexual assault, representing 28 percent of people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Sentence Lengths In 2020, most people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years, representing 36 percent of people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # Prison Releases ## Prison Release Trends From 2010 to 2020, prison releases increased 34 percent, dropping from 3,108 in 2010 to 4,156 in 2020. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
In 2020, 48 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 6 percent increase compared to 2010. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Conditional Releases Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2020, most people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic people, representing 65 percent of prison releases. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Gender In 2020, most people released from prison were males, representing 86 percent of prison releases. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: #### Age In 2020, most releases from prison were people between 25 to 34 years old, representing 36 percent of prison releases. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
## Offense Types In 2020, most people released from prison were incarcerated for property offenses, representing 25 percent of people. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # Disparities ## Disparities in Incarceration ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity Racial and ethnic disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/rri_infographic_black_Utah.png){width=1180} ::: ::: Black people are incarcerated in state prison at a rate 8.7 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Utah. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/rri_infographic_hispanic_Utah.png){width=1177} ::: ::: Hispanic people are incarcerated in state prison at a rate 1.9 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Utah. ### Gender Gender disparities in prison incarceration represents the accumulation of disparities across the system.
:::
## Time Served ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by race and ethnicity. The largest disparity was observed among negligent manslaughter offenses, where Black people spent 13.5 more years in prison on average compared to people of Other race(s), who had the shortest time served for these offenses. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Gender This chart shows the average time served in prison for the most serious sentenced offense by gender. On average, females spent 1 fewer years in prison compared to males in Utah. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: For murder and non-negligent manslaughter offenses, males spent 12.1 more years in prison on average compared to females. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: :::
## Disparities in Parole Eligibility ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pe_rri_infographic_black_Utah.png){width=1150} ::: ::: Black people are incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 6 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Utah. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pe_rri_infographic_hispanic_Utah.png){width=1181} ::: ::: Hispanic people are incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 2.1 times higher than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Utah. ### Gender :::
## Years in Prison After Parole Eligibility ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity Black people spent an average of 0.3 more years in prison past parole eligibility and Hispanic people spent an average of 0.6 more years in prison past parole eligibility compared to White people. ```{=html}

## How Disparities Are Calculated While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material. State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals. *States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.* :::
## End Notes Data analysis was conducted using information from the National Corrections Reporting Program and the National Prisoner Statistics Program. See “National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP),” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp; “National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) Program,” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-prisoner-statistics-nps-program.
